meta.title: Eduversal School Appraisal Framework
meta.description: A five-domain institutional self-appraisal framework for Eduversal partner schools. Aligned with CIS, WASC, NEASC, Danielson-informed practices, and Permendiknas No. 13/2007. Schools complete a self-assessment; external evaluators from Eduversal validate and provide commendations and recommendations.

references.name: WASC (Western Association of Schools and Colleges)
NEASC (New England Association of Schools and Colleges)

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Practice is absent, inconsistent, or at an early stage. Little or no evidence exists.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Practice is emerging and partially implemented. Some evidence exists but gaps remain.

3.label: Established
3.description: Practice is consistently implemented with clear evidence. Meets expected standards.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Practice is fully embedded, innovative, and can serve as a model for others.

meta.cycle: Annual self-appraisal by school, followed by external validation visit within the same academic year.
meta.source_document: Eduversal School Appraisal Handbook v1.0 — May 2025

domains.title: Leadership & Governance
domains.description: Evaluates the quality of school leadership, strategic planning, governance structures, legal compliance, and the use of data and evidence in decision-making. Strong leadership provides clear direction, builds institutional culture, and ensures accountability at all levels.
references: CIS School Evaluation — Governance and Leadership Standards
WASC Self-Study — Category A: Organization for Student Learning

aspects.title: Vision, Mission & Strategic Direction
aspects.description: The school has a clearly articulated vision and mission underpinned by core educational values. A School Development Plan with SMART goals and the P.I.E. model is in place and actively monitored by leadership.
expectations: The school has a clear mission and vision statement underpinned by educational values that are documented and publicly accessible.
The school actively promotes core educational values across all aspects of school life.
A strategic or long-range plan (Plan for Growth & Improvement) exists to improve student performance.
Development goals are set through genuine involvement of all stakeholders — parents, students, teachers, and leaders.
Goals are communicated clearly with all stakeholders through multiple channels.
A feasibility study is conducted for all development goals prior to implementation.
Goals are SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound).
Goals directly address teaching and learning outcomes as a priority.
evidence_examples: School development goals document
School Development Plan (P.I.E. model)
School Smart Goal documentation
Stakeholder consultation records (meeting minutes, surveys)
Feasibility study reports for development goals
Published vision and mission statements (website, handbook)

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Mission and vision exist on paper but are not embedded in practice. No strategic plan with SMART goals is evident. Stakeholder involvement is absent.

2.label: Developing
2.description: A strategic plan exists but goals are not fully SMART or stakeholder-informed. Feasibility studies are partial. Communication to stakeholders is inconsistent.

3.label: Established
3.description: Clear mission, vision, and SMART development goals are documented, communicated, and linked to teaching and learning outcomes. Stakeholders were meaningfully involved in goal setting.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: The school's mission and vision visibly drive all decisions. Goals are rigorously SMART, evidence-based, stakeholder-co-created, and monitored through a robust P.I.E. model cycle. The school is a model for strategic planning.

aspects.title: SMART Goals & P.I.E. Model Implementation
aspects.description: Leadership teams are established for all school development goals. Team members understand their roles and responsibilities, and all goals are monitored through action planning, the P.I.E. (Plan, Implement, Evaluate) model, and documented evidence.
expectations: Leadership teams are set for all school development goals, with clearly assigned membership.
Leadership team members know and can articulate their roles and responsibilities.
Leadership team members demonstrate qualities of being optimistic, proactive, and concerned for others.
Leadership teams make full preparation based on the P.I.E. model (Plan, Implement, Evaluate).
Goals are monitored through structured action planning with timelines, milestones, and responsible parties.
Progress toward goals is tracked through documented evidence at regular intervals.
evidence_examples: Leadership Team list with assigned goals
Job descriptions and role assignments for leadership team members
School Development Plan structured around P.I.E. model
Action plans with timelines and evidence checkpoints
Progress monitoring reports with evidence documentation
School Smart Goal tracking records

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Leadership teams are not formally assigned for development goals. P.I.E. model is not used. Action plans are absent or generic.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Leadership teams exist but roles are unclear. P.I.E. model is partially applied. Action plans exist but monitoring is irregular.

3.label: Established
3.description: Leadership teams are established with clear roles for all development goals. P.I.E. model guides implementation. Action planning and evidence collection are consistent.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Leadership teams operate with a high degree of autonomy and effectiveness. P.I.E. model is deeply embedded. Evidence collection is systematic and leads to real-time adjustments. Practices are documented and transferable.

aspects.title: School Leadership Structure & Shared Leadership
aspects.description: The school has an effective organisational structure that facilitates clear, consistent, and transparent communication. Leadership is distributed, with opportunities for staff at all levels to contribute to decision-making.
expectations: An organisational structure facilitates clear, consistent, and transparent communication across the school.
Effective internal communication processes are in place — regular meetings, clear protocols, and accessible platforms.
Roles and responsibilities are clearly defined in written policies and handbooks.
A culture of shared leadership exists, with genuine opportunities for staff to participate in decision-making.
Leadership proactively takes responsibility for creating a safe and secure learning environment.
Child protection procedures are established, communicated, and adhered to by all staff.
evidence_examples: School Organisational Chart
Staff/Employee Handbook
Parent-Student Handbook
Meeting schedules, agendas, and minutes demonstrating regular communication
Staff participation records in decision-making processes
Safeguarding & Child Protection Policy

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Organisational structure is unclear. Communication is ad hoc. Roles and responsibilities are not documented. Decision-making is top-down with no staff involvement.

2.label: Developing
2.description: An org chart exists but communication processes are inconsistent. Some roles are documented. Limited opportunities exist for shared leadership.

3.label: Established
3.description: Organisational structure is clear, roles are documented, and communication processes are effective. Staff have genuine opportunities to participate in decision-making. Child protection procedures are in place.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: The school is characterised by distributed leadership where staff at all levels feel empowered to contribute. Communication is transparent and multi-directional. Shared leadership produces measurable improvements in school culture and outcomes.

aspects.title: Performance Management & Staff KPIs
aspects.description: The school has a structured performance management system for all staff, including KPIs for leaders. Regular and constructive supervision and evaluation focus on professional growth and accountability.
expectations: Regular and constructive supervision and evaluation processes focus on teacher professional growth.
A culture of accountability, peer observation, mentoring, and shared decision-making is actively cultivated.
Ongoing support for professional learning is provided — research-based and with measurable impact.
A KPI system exists for evaluating principal and leadership team performance.
Evaluation processes are transparent and outcomes are used to inform improvement plans.
School leaders undergo their own evaluation — survey results and reports are documented.
evidence_examples: KPI System documentation for leaders and staff
Evaluation Survey and Report for School Leaders
Performance management policy and process documents
Professional development plans linked to evaluation outcomes
Most Recent School Annual Report
Leadership appraisal records

1.label: Beginning
1.description: No formal performance management system exists for staff or leaders. Evaluation is absent or informal. KPIs are not used.

2.label: Developing
2.description: A performance management system exists but is inconsistently applied. KPIs for leaders are defined but not rigorously tracked. Evaluation outcomes rarely drive improvement actions.

3.label: Established
3.description: A structured performance management system with KPIs is in place for all staff. Evaluation is constructive, growth-focused, and leads to documented improvement plans. Annual reports demonstrate accountability.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Performance management is a genuine driver of professional growth. KPIs are sophisticated, data-linked, and co-constructed with staff. Evaluation culture is positive — staff welcome feedback and the system demonstrably improves outcomes.

aspects.title: Governance, Compliance & Transparent Decision-Making
aspects.description: The school operates within a legal and regulatory framework. Governance is transparent and inclusive, with decisions informed by stakeholder data. The school adheres to all applicable national regulations and institutional requirements.
expectations: Transparent and inclusive decision-making is informed by stakeholder data from surveys, consultations, and feedback mechanisms.
The school adheres to all legal and regulatory requirements applicable to its operating context.
Official government documents, permits, and compliance records are maintained and up to date.
A governing body or foundation board provides oversight and holds leadership accountable.
Governance processes and major decisions are documented and communicated to relevant stakeholders.
evidence_examples: Official government documents (maintained in Edunav or equivalent system)
Institutional operating permits and licences
Governing body or foundation board meeting minutes
Stakeholder survey results used in governance decisions
Legal compliance records and policy register
School Academic Calendar (as a governance planning document)

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Governance structures are weak or informal. Legal compliance documentation is incomplete. Decision-making is opaque and not informed by stakeholder input.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Governance structures exist but oversight is inconsistent. Some legal compliance documentation is in place. Stakeholder feedback is occasionally consulted in decisions.

3.label: Established
3.description: The school operates within a clear governance framework. Legal and regulatory compliance is documented and current. Decision-making is informed by stakeholder data and is transparent.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Governance is a genuine strength — oversight is rigorous, stakeholders are meaningfully engaged, and the school anticipates and exceeds compliance requirements. Governance practices are a model of transparency and integrity.

domains.title: Teaching & Learning Quality
domains.description: Evaluates the quality of classroom practice, lesson planning, assessment methods, feedback quality, evaluation of teaching effectiveness, and the purposeful integration of technology and LMS in learning. Aligned with Danielson Framework domains 1–3.

aspects.title: In-Class Documents: Lesson Plans, Observations & Reflections
aspects.description: Teachers produce comprehensive, well-structured lesson and unit plans aligned to curriculum objectives. Classroom observations are systematically recorded, and teachers engage in regular reflections on their own practice.
expectations: Comprehensive, well-structured lesson and unit plans are produced with clear curriculum alignment, clear objectives, differentiation strategies, and assessment methods.
Detailed classroom observation records are maintained — capturing observations by peers, administrators, and external evaluators.
Teachers engage in regular, thoughtful reflections on their teaching practices — written and documented.
Lesson plans reference prior assessment data to inform current planning decisions.
Plans include strategies for diverse learners and demonstrate knowledge of students' needs.
evidence_examples: A1 Lesson Plans / Unit Plans
A2 Classroom Observations
A3 Teacher Reflections
Differentiation strategies documentation in lesson plans
Assessment-to-planning feedback cycles
Unit plans with curriculum alignment mapping

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Lesson plans are absent, incomplete, or generic. Classroom observations are not documented. Teacher reflections do not occur or are not recorded.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Lesson plans exist but lack differentiation or assessment integration. Observations are occasional. Reflections are surface-level and not acted upon.

3.label: Established
3.description: Well-structured lesson and unit plans are consistently produced with clear objectives, differentiation, and assessment methods. Observations are documented. Reflections are regular and inform future planning.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Lesson planning is exemplary — deeply differentiated, evidence-driven, and responsive to student data. Observations and reflections form a continuous improvement cycle. Teachers use reflections to adapt practice in real time.

sample_documents.label: A1 Lesson Plans and Unit Plans
Lesson Plan and LMS Observation Log

aspects.title: Assessment Methods
aspects.description: The school has clear, well-documented assessment policies. A variety of assessment types — formative, summative, and diagnostic — are used. Assessment data is comprehensively collected, analysed, and used to inform instruction.
expectations: Clear, well-documented assessment policies exist that define grading criteria, frequency, and curriculum alignment.
A variety of assessment types are used — formative, summative, and diagnostic.
Comprehensive collection and analysis of assessment data is conducted, with trends identified and used to inform instruction.
Well-organised student portfolios are maintained across subjects, demonstrating individual growth over time.
Assessment policies are communicated to students and parents and are consistently applied across subjects.
evidence_examples: B1 Assessment Policies
B2 Sample Assessments (formative, summative, diagnostic)
B3 Assessment Data (trend analysis, grade distributions)
C1 Student Portfolios
Moderation records demonstrating consistency across teachers
Student self-assessment and peer feedback samples

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Assessment policies are absent or not documented. Only summative assessments are used. Data collection is ad hoc and not used to inform teaching.

2.label: Developing
2.description: An assessment policy exists but variety of assessment types is limited. Data is collected but analysis is inconsistent. Portfolios are incomplete or not maintained.

3.label: Established
3.description: Clear assessment policies are in place and communicated. Formative, summative, and diagnostic assessments are used. Data informs instruction. Portfolios demonstrate student growth.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Assessment is a defining strength. Policy drives a rich ecosystem of assessment types. Data analysis is sophisticated and school-wide. Portfolios are exemplary artefacts of student learning and are used in student-led conferences.

aspects.title: Student Understanding & Goal Setting
aspects.description: Students are actively involved in understanding their own learning progress. They set academic and personal goals, participate in goal review check-ins, and take ownership of their learning through student-led conferences and portfolios.
expectations: Student involvement in setting academic and personal goals is documented, with regular check-ins by teachers or tutors.
Student-led conferences are held and document student ownership of learning.
Students can articulate their progress, strengths, and areas for improvement.
Goal-setting documents are maintained and reviewed throughout the year.
Students receive constructive, specific feedback on assignments and assessments that supports their goal progress.
evidence_examples: C2 Goal Setting Documents
C3 Student-Led Conference documentation
E1 Feedback Samples (written feedback on student work)
Student reflection records and self-assessment forms
Tutor or pastoral check-in logs
Student portfolio entries with growth annotations

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Students have no involvement in goal-setting. Feedback is generic or infrequent. Student-led conferences do not occur.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Goal-setting activities occur but are not consistently followed up. Feedback exists but is not always specific or actionable. Student-led conferences are occasional.

3.label: Established
3.description: Students regularly set goals with teacher support and receive specific, developmental feedback. Student-led conferences occur and document student ownership. Check-ins are scheduled and documented.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Students are highly self-regulated learners who articulate their goals confidently and track their own progress. Feedback loops are closed — students respond to feedback and improve. Student-led conferences are a school highlight.

aspects.title: Assessment & Feedback Quality
aspects.description: Constructive and specific feedback is provided to students on assignments and assessments. The effectiveness of assessment and feedback practices is reviewed regularly through surveys and structured review meetings.
expectations: Constructive, specific feedback is provided to students on assignments and assessments in a timely manner.
Student, parent, and teacher survey results on assessment and feedback effectiveness are collected and acted upon.
Regular review meetings on assessment practices and feedback effectiveness are held and documented.
Students have opportunities to respond to feedback and close the feedback loop.
Book scrutiny or work sampling is used to monitor the quality of feedback given across the school.
evidence_examples: E1 Feedback Samples (annotated student work)
E2 Survey Results (student/parent/teacher feedback on assessment effectiveness)
E3 Review Meeting minutes on assessment and feedback practices
Book scrutiny or work sampling records
Student work samples showing response to feedback

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Feedback to students is rare, generic, or not timely. No review of feedback practices occurs. Survey data on feedback is not collected.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Feedback is provided but varies in quality across teachers. Review meetings occur occasionally. Survey data is collected but rarely used to improve practice.

3.label: Established
3.description: Specific and timely feedback is consistently provided. Review meetings on feedback quality are held regularly. Survey data informs practice. Book scrutiny provides quality assurance.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Feedback culture is exceptional — specific, developmental, and dialogic. Students routinely act on feedback. Feedback quality is monitored rigorously and continuously improves. Survey data drives school-wide assessment innovation.

aspects.title: Evaluation of Teaching & Professional Development Records
aspects.description: Teaching is systematically evaluated through professional development records, peer review, and individualised improvement plans. Evidence of PD impact on classroom practice is maintained.
expectations: Comprehensive records of teacher professional development participation are maintained, with reflections on impact in the classroom.
Peer review reports offering constructive feedback on teaching practices are documented.
Individualised teacher improvement plans are developed based on evaluation findings.
Professional development is aligned to school improvement priorities and is not ad hoc.
PD impact is measured — teachers apply new learning and its effect on student outcomes is tracked.
evidence_examples: F1 Professional Development Records (attendance, topics, reflections)
F2 Peer Review Reports
F3 Improvement Plans (individualised, based on evaluation)
PD calendar and post-PD impact reflection forms
Teacher appraisal records linking PD to classroom outcomes
School professional development impact report

1.label: Beginning
1.description: PD records are not maintained. Peer review does not occur. Improvement plans are absent. PD is ad hoc with no evidence of impact.

2.label: Developing
2.description: PD records exist but reflections on impact are surface-level. Peer review is occasional. Improvement plans exist for some teachers but not consistently.

3.label: Established
3.description: Comprehensive PD records with impact reflections are maintained. Peer review is embedded in practice. Individualised improvement plans are developed and monitored.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: PD is a strategic investment with clear impact on student outcomes. Peer review is a celebrated professional norm. Improvement plans are dynamic and produce measurable classroom improvement. The school can demonstrate the ROI of its PD programme.

aspects.title: LMS & Technology Integration in Teaching
aspects.description: The school uses an LMS actively with teachers, students, and parents. Educational technology is purposefully integrated into teaching to support diverse instructional strategies and enhance learning objectives. Innovative practices such as flipped classrooms and blended learning are observed.
expectations: Students, teachers, and parents actively participate using the school's Learning Management System (LMS).
LMS content is effectively organised, regularly updated, and aligned with curriculum standards.
Adequate and effective training is provided for teachers, students, and parents on LMS use.
Technology enhances learning objectives and supports diverse instructional strategies — not used as a substitution only.
Student engagement and interaction with technology is at a meaningful and appropriate level.
Innovative teaching practices — flipped classrooms, blended learning, project-based learning — are observed and documented.
Technology is used in formative and summative assessments (online quizzes, digital portfolios, interactive assessments) with timely feedback.
evidence_examples: LMS analytics and report (active courses, submission rates, engagement metrics)
LMS policy for teachers and course content documentation
IHT (In-House Training) records for new teachers on LMS
Technology inventory
Training documentation for LMS onboarding
Lesson plans and materials showing technology integration
LMS observation records

1.label: Beginning
1.description: LMS is either absent or rarely used. Technology use in teaching is minimal or purely substitutive. No training for staff on technology integration.

2.label: Developing
2.description: LMS is used but participation is low or inconsistent. Technology is used but does not meaningfully enhance learning. Training is occasional.

3.label: Established
3.description: LMS is actively used by teachers, students, and parents with organised content. Technology enhances learning objectives. Regular training is provided. Innovative practices are beginning to be observed.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: LMS is a central pillar of teaching and learning. Technology is pedagogically sophisticated — enhancing, transforming, and innovating learning experiences. Flipped learning, blended models, and digital assessment are standard practice. Data from LMS informs teaching decisions.

domains.title: Curriculum & Academic Programmes
domains.description: Evaluates the organisation and effectiveness of subject departments, the quality of curriculum delivery, professional collaboration among teachers, formal observations and walkthroughs, mentoring and peer observation systems, and the integration of cultural contexts and digital policy in curriculum design.
references: Cambridge International — School Quality and Curriculum Framework
CIS Standards for Curriculum, Instruction and Assessment

aspects.title: Subject Department Structure & Organisation
aspects.description: Each subject department is well-organised with a maintained member list, a qualified and experienced Head of Department, and clear departmental responsibilities that support curriculum coherence and student outcomes.
expectations: An updated list of department members is maintained and accessible.
A qualified teacher — preferably with mentorship qualifications — is appointed as Head of Department.
Each HoD has clearly defined roles and responsibilities documented in writing.
Departmental structures facilitate curriculum coherence across year groups and subjects.
Department improvement plans are linked to school-wide strategic development goals.
evidence_examples: School subject departments info sheet (department members and meeting minutes link)
HoD appointment letters and qualification records
Job descriptions for Heads of Department
Department improvement plans
School Organisational Chart showing departmental structure

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Department structures are informal or undocumented. HoD roles are unclear. Member lists are not maintained. No link between department plans and school strategy.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Department structures exist but documentation is incomplete. HoDs are appointed but their roles are not fully defined. Department plans exist but are loosely linked to school goals.

3.label: Established
3.description: Each department has updated member lists, a qualified HoD with defined responsibilities, and improvement plans linked to school goals. Structures support curriculum coherence.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Departments are high-performing collaborative units. HoDs are instructional leaders who drive curriculum innovation. Departmental improvement plans are sophisticated, evidence-driven, and produce measurable outcomes.

sample_documents.label: Sample school subject departments information sheet template

aspects.title: Department Meetings & HoD Coordination
aspects.description: Subject department meetings are held regularly on a biweekly basis and focus on student outcomes. HoD coordination meetings led by the principal ensure school-wide curriculum coherence. All meetings are formally minuted.
expectations: Department meetings are held biweekly, spanning two lessons per meeting.
Meeting minutes are recorded and maintained for all subject departments.
Student outcomes are at the centre of meeting discussions — improving teaching, curriculum, assessment, and interventions.
HoD coordination meetings are led by the principal and held biweekly.
Meeting minutes for HoD coordination meetings are recorded and maintained.
Action items from meetings are tracked and followed up at subsequent meetings.
evidence_examples: Subject department meeting minutes (biweekly)
HoD coordination meeting minutes (biweekly, led by principal)
Meeting attendance records
Action item tracking logs from meeting minutes
Evidence of student outcome discussions within meeting records

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Department meetings are infrequent or unminuted. HoD coordination meetings do not occur. Student outcomes are not discussed in meetings.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Meetings occur but not on a consistent biweekly schedule. Minutes are sometimes recorded. Student outcomes are discussed occasionally. HoD coordination is irregular.

3.label: Established
3.description: Department meetings occur biweekly with recorded minutes focused on student outcomes. HoD coordination meetings are held regularly by the principal and are well-documented.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Meetings are exemplary professional learning communities. Minutes evidence deep curriculum discussion, action-oriented decisions, and accountability follow-up. HoD coordination drives school-wide curriculum coherence and improvement.

aspects.title: Teacher Self-Assessment, Goal Setting & Action Plans
aspects.description: All teachers engage in structured self-assessment, identifying strengths, areas for improvement, professional development goals, and action plans. School leaders periodically review and support teachers' yearly goals.
expectations: Teachers engage in self-assessment using structured forms that capture strengths, areas for improvement, PD goals, and an action plan.
Self-assessment forms are completed at the start of each academic year and reviewed mid-year.
School leaders — principal, AP, HoD — periodically review teachers' yearly goals, self-assessments, and action plans.
Goal-setting is aligned to both personal professional development and school improvement priorities.
Action plans include specific, time-bound steps and are revisited in formal review conversations.
evidence_examples: Teachers development documents (self-evaluations, goals, action plan, report)
Self-assessment forms completed by teachers
Goal-setting records with mid-year review notes
Leader review meeting records for teacher goals
Action plan templates and completed examples

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Teacher self-assessment does not occur or is not documented. Goals are absent. Leaders do not review teacher development documents.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Self-assessment forms exist and are completed by some teachers. Goals are set but not regularly reviewed. Leader involvement in reviewing goals is inconsistent.

3.label: Established
3.description: All teachers complete structured self-assessments with goals and action plans. School leaders review these regularly. Goals are aligned to both personal growth and school priorities.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Self-assessment and goal-setting are deeply embedded in professional culture. Teachers are highly self-aware and proactive. Leaders engage in rich professional dialogue about teacher growth. Action plans produce demonstrable impact on teaching quality.

sample_documents.label: Template and Sample Teacher PD Plan

aspects.title: Formal Observations & Walkthroughs
aspects.description: Teachers receive regular formal observations and frequent walkthroughs by leadership. Observations use the Edunav Appraisal system, involve multiple observers, and result in documented developmental feedback.
expectations: Formal observations are conducted at least 3 times per semester for each teacher, by different leadership members — principal, AP, HoD, GM — using Edunav Appraisal.
Frequent walkthroughs by the principal occur at least once per week per classroom and are documented.
Observation records include specific developmental feedback linked to teaching standards.
Post-observation conversations take place and are recorded.
Observation data is used collectively to identify school-wide patterns in teaching quality and inform PD priorities.
evidence_examples: Appraisal records in Edunav (https://appraisal.zerone.id/)
Walkthrough observation log (principal, weekly)
Formal observation schedules showing distribution across observers
Post-observation feedback records
Aggregate observation data used for PD planning

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Formal observations are rare or undocumented. Walkthroughs do not occur regularly. Observation feedback is absent or superficial.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Formal observations occur but fewer than 3 per semester. Walkthroughs are irregular. Feedback exists but post-observation dialogue does not consistently occur.

3.label: Established
3.description: Formal observations (min. 3/semester) are conducted by multiple leaders using Edunav. Weekly walkthroughs occur and are logged. Developmental feedback and post-observation conversations are documented.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Observations and walkthroughs are embedded as a positive professional growth culture. Observation data drives PD planning and curriculum decisions. Teachers actively seek feedback and demonstrate growth between cycles.

aspects.title: Mentoring Programme & Peer Observation System
aspects.description: An active mentoring programme pairs experienced teachers with newer colleagues. A structured peer observation system ensures that all teachers observe and are observed by peers regularly, with frequency adjusted for less experienced teachers.
expectations: A mentoring programme pairs experienced teachers with new colleagues — the principal assigns mentors for all first-year teachers.
Mentor-mentee activities are logged and documented throughout the year.
A peer observation system is in place where each teacher observes at least one other teacher per month.
Less experienced teachers participate in peer observation with greater frequency than more experienced colleagues.
Peer observation records are maintained and used to support professional dialogue and growth.
evidence_examples: Mentor-mentee list and mentorship activity log
Teachers peer observation log
Peer observation forms with feedback
Principal assignment records for first-year teacher mentors
Mentoring programme policy or guidelines document

1.label: Beginning
1.description: No mentoring programme exists. Peer observation does not occur or is undocumented. New teachers receive no structured support.

2.label: Developing
2.description: A mentoring programme exists informally. Peer observations occur occasionally but are not systematic. Logs are incomplete.

3.label: Established
3.description: A structured mentoring programme pairs experienced and new teachers. Peer observations occur at least monthly per teacher with logs maintained. Less experienced teachers receive more frequent observation support.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Mentoring and peer observation are hallmarks of the school's professional culture. Mentors are skilled coaches. Peer observation produces documented professional growth. The system is self-sustaining and teachers actively value it.

aspects.title: Evaluation of Professional Development Activities
aspects.description: The effectiveness of professional development activities is systematically evaluated through feedback and learning outcomes. Evaluation findings are used to plan and improve future PD activities.
expectations: All PD activities are evaluated for effectiveness through participant feedback and measurable outcomes.
Evaluation data is analysed and used to plan future professional development programmes.
The school produces a professional development impact report documenting outcomes and improvements.
PD evaluation is tied to both teacher growth goals and school improvement priorities.
Teachers document how PD learning has been applied in their classroom practice.
evidence_examples: School professional development impact report
PD activity feedback forms and analysis
PD planning documents showing use of evaluation findings
Teacher application reports linking PD to classroom practice
Year-end evaluation report on PD programme effectiveness

1.label: Beginning
1.description: PD activities are not evaluated. No impact data is collected. Future PD planning is not informed by evidence.

2.label: Developing
2.description: PD feedback forms are used but analysis is superficial. Impact on teaching is not tracked. PD planning is only partially informed by evaluation findings.

3.label: Established
3.description: PD activities are evaluated through feedback and outcomes. A PD impact report is produced. Findings inform future PD planning. Teachers document application of learning.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: PD evaluation is rigorous and sophisticated — connecting teacher learning to student outcomes. The impact report demonstrates clear ROI. A continuous improvement cycle in PD design and delivery is evident.

sample_documents.label: School professional development impact report template

aspects.title: Cultural & Authentic Contexts, Use of Resources
aspects.description: The curriculum incorporates diverse cultural perspectives and authentic learning contexts. Learning resources — including textbooks, digital tools, and community connections — are selected purposefully and reviewed for effectiveness.
expectations: Curriculum maps incorporate diverse cultural perspectives and authentic learning contexts.
Student work samples demonstrate engagement with culturally diverse content.
Field trips and guest speaker events are documented with clear objectives, reflections, and curriculum connections.
Up-to-date inventories of learning resources — textbooks, digital tools — are maintained with usage data.
Plans for integrating technology into instruction include rationale, strategies, and outcomes.
Feedback from students and teachers on learning resource effectiveness and accessibility is collected and used.
evidence_examples: L1 Curriculum Maps (incorporating cultural perspectives)
L2 Student Work Samples (culturally diverse content engagement)
L3 Field Trip and Guest Speaker Logs (objectives, reflections, curriculum connections)
H1 Resource Inventories (textbooks, digital tools, usage data)
H2 Technology Integration Plans
H3 Student and Teacher Feedback on resources

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Curriculum does not reflect cultural diversity. Resource inventories are absent. Field trips lack curriculum connections. Technology integration is not planned.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Some cultural content is present in the curriculum. Resource inventories exist but are outdated. Field trips occur but are not well-documented. Technology integration is ad hoc.

3.label: Established
3.description: Curriculum maps include diverse cultural perspectives. Resource inventories are current. Field trips and guest speakers are well-documented with curriculum connections. Technology integration is planned and reviewed.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Authentic, culturally rich learning is a defining feature of the curriculum. Resources are curated with precision. Field trips and community connections transform learning. Student work demonstrates deep cultural engagement and real-world application.

aspects.title: Digital Policy
aspects.description: The school has a comprehensive and up-to-date digital policy that governs technology use in teaching, learning, and administration. The policy is communicated to all stakeholders, promotes digital citizenship, and is reviewed to address emerging technologies.
expectations: The school's digital policy is available and covers guidelines for technology use in teaching, learning, and administration.
Digital policy is communicated to and understood by teachers, students, and parents.
Programmes promoting digital citizenship and ethical use of technology are in place and delivered to students.
The school monitors and updates its digital policy to address emerging technologies and evolving risks.
Communication records show that all stakeholders have been informed of digital policy requirements.
evidence_examples: Digital Policy document (Internet/Social Media and Digital Learning policy)
Communication records showing policy distribution to teachers, students, parents
Digital citizenship programme documentation
Policy review records with version history and update dates
Feedback from stakeholders on digital policy understanding

1.label: Beginning
1.description: No digital policy exists. Digital citizenship is not taught. Technology use is unregulated.

2.label: Developing
2.description: A digital policy exists but is not well-communicated or consistently applied. Digital citizenship is addressed informally. Policy has not been recently reviewed.

3.label: Established
3.description: A clear digital policy is in place, communicated to all stakeholders, and consistently applied. A digital citizenship programme is delivered. The policy is reviewed annually.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: The digital policy is exemplary — comprehensive, proactively updated, and deeply understood by all stakeholders. Digital citizenship is woven throughout the curriculum. The school anticipates emerging technology risks and responds proactively.

domains.title: Student Achievement & Learning Outcomes
domains.description: Evaluates how well students are learning, how outcomes are tracked and reported, the effectiveness of the Olympiad programme and enrichment activities, and how the school supports students with diverse learning needs — including advanced learners, language learners, and students with difficulties.
references: Cambridge International — Exam Results Analysis Guidance
WASC Self-Study — Category B: Student Learning

aspects.title: Student Achievement Data & Dashboards
aspects.description: The school tracks student achievement data systematically through user-friendly dashboards. Regular detailed progress reports are produced, and in-depth analysis at individual, class, and school levels is used to drive improvement.
expectations: User-friendly, up-to-date dashboards for student achievement data are maintained and regularly reviewed.
Regular, detailed progress reports are produced covering achievement, areas for improvement, and next steps.
In-depth analysis of achievement data occurs at individual, class, and school levels.
Academic performance data is collected and analysed at least once per term.
Stakeholder feedback — surveys from parents, students, and staff — is gathered and used to inform decisions.
Data is used to set targets, identify gaps, and monitor progress over time.
evidence_examples: D1 Data Dashboards (student achievement, real-time or termly)
D2 Progress Reports (termly, individual student)
D3 Data Analysis Reports (individual, class, school level)
Academic performance dashboards or trend reports
Parent and student satisfaction survey results and follow-up actions
Attendance and pastoral intervention records linked to data

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Achievement data is not systematically tracked. Dashboards are absent. Progress reports are generic or infrequent. Data analysis does not occur.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Data is collected but dashboards are basic or not regularly updated. Progress reports exist but lack detail. Analysis is limited to overall school level.

3.label: Established
3.description: Up-to-date dashboards track achievement. Detailed progress reports are produced regularly. In-depth analysis at individual, class, and school levels informs decisions.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Data systems are highly sophisticated — real-time dashboards, predictive analytics, and multi-level analysis are standard. Progress reports are exemplary communication tools. Data drives a genuine improvement culture.

aspects.title: Olympiad Programme: Resources & Academic Follow-Up
aspects.description: The school's Olympiad programme is well-resourced, strategically structured, and aligned with the school's mission. Students are selected through a rigorous process, follow a study plan, and receive regular assessments and academic follow-up throughout the year.
expectations: The aims of the Olympiad Team match the school's mission and vision.
Students are selected through a rigorous selection process that is documented and transparent.
Olympiad Team regulations are regularly revised to remain current and effective.
Qualified tutors are hired and assigned to relevant subjects.
A syllabus and study plan are in place and followed regularly.
Resources — reading materials, question banks — are selected according to specific needs of students and teachers.
Planning, implementation, and follow-ups for regular assessments including Olympiad mock exams are documented.
Olympiad student performances are monitored throughout the year, with interventions made as needed.
All programmes are well-documented — programme descriptions, conduct records, evaluations — and reports are updated regularly.
Students are given structured feedback on their performance reports.
evidence_examples: Handbook of Olympiad Team
Competition list and Achievement list
Student target documentation
Study plan and reading materials / question banks
Meeting notes with tutors and management
Tutoring session schedule and attendance records
Follow-up report (Progress Card)
Olympiad student performance report
Year-end evaluation report

1.label: Beginning
1.description: No structured Olympiad programme exists. Selection is informal. Study plans and resources are absent. Performance is not monitored.

2.label: Developing
2.description: An Olympiad programme exists but is poorly structured. Study plans are informal. Monitoring is inconsistent. Documentation is incomplete.

3.label: Established
3.description: The Olympiad programme is well-structured with rigorous selection, qualified tutors, a study plan, regular assessments, and performance monitoring. Documentation is comprehensive.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: The Olympiad programme is a flagship of academic excellence — meticulously planned, rigorously executed, and producing consistent achievement results. Data-driven follow-up and individualised student support distinguish it as a model programme.

aspects.title: Olympiad Programme: Student Well-being Management
aspects.description: The well-being of Olympiad students is actively planned and managed in partnership with the relevant school unit. Regular well-being activities, personal goal development, and career planning ensure that academic excellence does not come at the cost of student health.
expectations: Student well-being is planned in coordination with the relevant school unit and progress is followed up.
Well-being activities are carried out regularly for Olympiad students.
Students' personal goals are developed and followed up throughout the year.
Well-being goals and career planning are regularly reviewed and documented.
Scholarship students and medal-projected students are followed closely with individualised support.
Meeting notes with students on well-being and personal goals are maintained.
evidence_examples: Well-being activity schedules and attendance records
Meeting notes with Olympiad students on goals and well-being
Personal goal development records
Career planning documentation for Olympiad students
Scholarship and medal-projected student follow-up records
Refreshment schedule and year-end evaluation report

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Student well-being in the Olympiad programme is not planned or monitored. No activities or personal goal development occur.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Some well-being activities occur but are not structured. Personal goals are set informally. Scholarship students receive limited specific follow-up.

3.label: Established
3.description: Well-being is systematically planned for Olympiad students. Regular activities occur. Personal goals and career planning are documented and reviewed. High-priority students are closely monitored.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: The Olympiad well-being model is holistic and exemplary. Students thrive academically and personally. Personal development, career planning, and academic preparation are seamlessly integrated. The school is a model for supporting high-achieving students sustainably.

aspects.title: Support for Advanced Students
aspects.description: The school identifies and provides differentiated, enriching support for advanced and gifted students. Enrichment programmes, differentiation strategies, and achievement data are used to ensure advanced students reach their full potential.
expectations: Documentation of differentiation strategies for advanced students is maintained and applied in teaching.
Records of enrichment programmes are kept, including participation rates, outcomes, and academic impact.
Achievement data analysis for advanced students is conducted to ensure continued progress and challenge.
Advanced students are challenged through extension tasks and enrichment opportunities beyond the standard curriculum.
Teachers demonstrate knowledge of and responsiveness to the needs of advanced learners in lesson planning and delivery.
evidence_examples: I1 Differentiation Strategies documentation (advanced learners)
I2 Enrichment Programme Records (participation, outcomes, academic impact)
I3 Student Achievement Data for advanced learners
Advanced learner tracking and progress reports
Extension task samples and teacher planning notes for gifted students

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Advanced students are not identified or given differentiated support. Enrichment programmes do not exist. Achievement data for this group is not analysed.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Some enrichment opportunities exist but are not systematic. Differentiation for advanced learners is inconsistent across teachers. Achievement data analysis is limited.

3.label: Established
3.description: Advanced students are identified and supported through documented differentiation strategies and enrichment programmes. Achievement data analysis confirms continued progress and challenge.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: The school excels at nurturing advanced learners. Enrichment programmes are sophisticated and produce outstanding outcomes. Differentiation is seamless. Advanced student achievement data demonstrates exceptional growth and impact.

aspects.title: Language Support for Students
aspects.description: The school provides individualised language support for non-native speakers and students whose home language differs from the language of instruction. Language development and academic performance are regularly assessed and tracked.
expectations: Individualised language support plans exist for non-native or EAL/ELL students.
Documentation of teacher qualifications in supporting language learners is maintained.
Regular assessments track language development and academic performance for supported students.
Language support is integrated with mainstream curriculum delivery — not isolated.
Student progress in language development is communicated to parents regularly.
evidence_examples: J1 Language Support Plans (individualised, per student)
J2 Teacher Qualifications documentation (language support competency)
J3 Progress Monitoring records (language development and academic performance)
EAL/ELL student tracking data
Language support intervention records and outcomes

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Language support needs are not identified. No support plans exist. Teacher qualifications in this area are not documented.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Some language support is provided but not systematically planned. Progress monitoring is irregular. Teacher qualifications are not consistently documented.

3.label: Established
3.description: Individualised language support plans are in place. Qualified staff deliver support. Progress in language development and academic performance is regularly assessed and communicated.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Language support is highly responsive and effective. Plans are individualised, rigorous, and reviewed frequently. Teacher expertise is high. EAL students achieve at or near the level of native speakers within a defined timeline.

aspects.title: Identification of Learning Needs & Support for Students with Difficulties
aspects.description: The school has systematic processes for identifying students with additional learning needs. Comprehensive documentation of special education needs, individual support plans, and intervention logs are maintained. Specialist reports are used to inform support strategies.
expectations: Thorough admissions records capture prior performance, learning needs, and background for all students.
Comprehensive documentation of special education needs exists — including IEPs, accommodation strategies, and support plans.
Detailed support logs record interventions, accommodations, and their effectiveness.
Specialist reports from psychologists and speech therapists are used to inform individual support strategies.
Feedback from students and parents on support effectiveness is collected and used to improve provision.
A process exists for identifying students with additional needs at the point of entry and throughout their schooling.
evidence_examples: G1 Admissions Records (prior performance, learning needs, background)
G2 Special Education Needs documentation (IEPs, accommodation strategies)
G3 Student Support Logs (interventions, accommodations, effectiveness)
K1 Specialist Reports (psychologists, speech therapists)
K2 Intervention Logs (frequency, duration, effectiveness)
K3 Students and Parents Feedback on support effectiveness

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Learning needs are not systematically identified. IEPs and support plans do not exist. Specialist reports are not used. Intervention logs are absent.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Some identification processes exist but are inconsistent. IEPs are in place for some students. Intervention logs are incomplete. Specialist input is occasional.

3.label: Established
3.description: A systematic process for identifying learning needs is in place from admissions onward. IEPs, specialist reports, and intervention logs are maintained. Parent and student feedback informs provision.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: The school is a model of inclusive practice. Learning needs are identified early and supported with precision. IEPs are exemplary and regularly reviewed. Specialist collaboration is embedded. Students with difficulties demonstrate measurable progress.

domains.title: School Culture & Community
domains.description: Evaluates the quality of the school's culture and climate, the effectiveness of internal communication and organisational management, safeguarding and child protection, parent and community engagement, educational programmes and activities, and staff well-being.
references: CIS Standards for School Culture and Community
WASC Self-Study — Category C: School Culture
Permendiknas No. 13/2007 — Social Competency Standards for Principals

aspects.title: School Culture, Climate & Student Behaviour
aspects.description: Students demonstrate behaviours consistent with the school's mission and values. The school has a positive, respectful, and inclusive culture in which all members of the community feel safe, valued, and engaged.
expectations: Students demonstrate behaviours consistent with the school's mission and values in daily school life.
A positive school culture is observable — in classrooms, corridors, and community interactions.
Clear policies for student behaviour and safety are in place, communicated, and consistently enforced.
Behaviour management data is tracked and used to identify patterns and inform interventions.
A culture of mutual respect among students, staff, and parents is actively cultivated and maintained.
evidence_examples: Behaviour Management System documentation
Parent-Student Handbook (student conduct section)
Behaviour incident data and trend analysis
Student and parent satisfaction survey results (culture and climate section)
School-wide values documentation and how they are embedded in daily life

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Behaviour policies are absent or not enforced. Student conduct is inconsistent with school values. No data on behaviour incidents is maintained. School culture is not actively cultivated.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Behaviour policies exist but are inconsistently applied. Some alignment between student behaviour and school values, but this is uneven. Culture is positive in some areas but not school-wide.

3.label: Established
3.description: Clear behaviour policies are in place and consistently applied. Students generally demonstrate conduct aligned with school values. Culture is positive and inclusive. Behaviour data is tracked and used.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: School culture is a genuine strength — observable in every interaction. Students embody school values authentically. Behaviour is self-regulated. The school is a community where every member feels valued and empowered.

aspects.title: Safeguarding & Child Protection
aspects.description: The school has robust safeguarding and child protection systems that protect the physical, emotional, and digital safety of all students. Background checks, mandatory training, and a code of conduct are in place for all staff.
expectations: Robust child protection safeguards are in place — including background checks for all staff, mandatory training, and a clear code of conduct.
A Safeguarding and Child Protection Policy is current, documented, and reviewed annually.
All staff receive safeguarding training on induction and annually thereafter.
A clear reporting and escalation procedure for safeguarding concerns is documented and understood by all staff.
Site security, visitor management, and online safety protocols are implemented and monitored.
Emergency and crisis management procedures are documented and regularly practised.
evidence_examples: Safeguarding & Child Protection Policy (with last review date)
Emergency / Crisis Manual
Staff background check records (maintained confidentially)
Mandatory safeguarding training attendance records
Reporting and escalation procedure flowchart
Site security log and visitor sign-in records

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Safeguarding policy is absent or outdated. Staff training does not occur. Background checks are not in place. Reporting procedures are unknown to staff.

2.label: Developing
2.description: A safeguarding policy exists but is not recently reviewed. Staff training is inconsistent. Reporting procedures exist but not all staff are aware of them. Background checks are partial.

3.label: Established
3.description: A current safeguarding policy is in place. All staff receive regular training. Background checks are conducted. Clear reporting procedures are known and followed. Emergency procedures are documented.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Safeguarding is a school-wide priority embedded in culture, not just compliance. Staff are confident and proactive in safeguarding roles. Systems are reviewed regularly and exceed minimum requirements. The school is a demonstrably safe environment for all students.

aspects.title: Communication Structures & Organisational Management
aspects.description: The school has effective internal and external communication processes. An organisational structure with clear roles and accessible information platforms ensures that all stakeholders — staff, students, and parents — are well-informed and connected.
expectations: An organisational structure facilitates clear, consistent, and transparent communication at all levels.
Effective internal communication processes are operational — regular meetings, clear protocols, and accessible digital platforms.
External communication to parents and the community is regular, clear, and multi-channel.
A School Academic Calendar is maintained, communicated to all stakeholders, and followed.
Staff, students, and parents can access relevant information through clear and accessible channels.
evidence_examples: School Organisational Chart
School Academic Calendar
Internal communication platform evidence (email, app, notice boards)
Parent communication samples (newsletters, circulars, app messages)
Meeting protocols and communication policy documentation

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Communication is ad hoc and inconsistent. No org chart. Academic calendar is not maintained or communicated. Parents receive infrequent updates.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Communication structures exist but are inconsistently used. Org chart is outdated. Academic calendar is communicated but not always followed. Parent communication is irregular.

3.label: Established
3.description: Clear communication structures are in place and operational. Org chart is current. Academic calendar is maintained and communicated. Parent communication is regular and multi-channel.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Communication is a school strength. Information flows efficiently and transparently across all levels. Stakeholders are well-informed, engaged, and report high satisfaction with school communication.

aspects.title: Educational Programmes & Co-Curricular Activities
aspects.description: The school promotes high-quality educational programmes and co-curricular activities for all students. Leadership tracks individual growth through these programmes and ensures all students have access to meaningful learning experiences beyond the classroom.
expectations: Leadership promotes high-quality educational programmes that are accessible to all students.
Individual student growth through educational programmes is tracked and reported.
A range of co-curricular activities — academic, creative, sporting, cultural, and service — is offered.
Student participation in co-curricular activities is tracked and reported.
Programmes are reviewed annually for relevance, quality, and impact on student development.
evidence_examples: Curriculum Documentation for all educational programmes
Co-curricular programme calendar and activity list
Student participation data in educational programmes and activities
Programme review records
Student achievement and growth data linked to specific programmes

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Co-curricular and broader educational programme offerings are minimal. Participation is not tracked. No review of programme quality occurs.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Some co-curricular programmes exist but offerings are limited. Participation data is collected inconsistently. Programme quality review is informal.

3.label: Established
3.description: A range of educational programmes and co-curricular activities is offered and accessible. Participation is tracked. Individual growth through programmes is monitored. Annual review occurs.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Educational programmes are distinctive, high-quality, and deeply valued by the school community. Participation is broad and inclusive. Growth through programmes is evidenced by data. Innovation in programme design is a school hallmark.

aspects.title: Parent & Community Engagement
aspects.description: The school actively involves parents and the wider community as partners in education. Transparent communication, meaningful participation opportunities, and formal representative structures ensure that families feel welcomed, informed, and engaged.
expectations: The school communicates regularly with parents through multiple channels — newsletters, app, parent portal, and meetings.
Parent-teacher conferences are held at least twice per year and are structured and productive.
A parent satisfaction survey is conducted annually and results are used to inform school actions.
Parents have a formal representative body — POMG or school committee — with a clear and active role.
Parents from diverse backgrounds feel welcomed and included in school life.
Community partnerships are in place and linked to student learning outcomes.
evidence_examples: Parent communication samples (newsletters, app messages, circulars)
Parent-teacher conference schedule and participation records
Parent satisfaction survey results and follow-up improvement actions
POMG / parent committee meeting minutes
Parent engagement events calendar and attendance records
Community partnership documentation (MoUs or letters of agreement)

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Parent communication is infrequent and one-directional. No parent satisfaction survey is conducted. No formal parent representative body exists. Community engagement is absent.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Parent-teacher conferences occur but are not well-structured. Communication is inconsistent. A parent body exists but is inactive. Community partnerships are minimal.

3.label: Established
3.description: Regular multi-channel communication keeps parents informed. Conferences are structured and productive. A parent satisfaction survey informs actions. A formal parent body is active. Community partnerships are in place.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Parents are genuine partners in the school community. Engagement is authentic, two-directional, and impactful. Survey findings drive real change. The parent body is influential. Community partnerships enrich student learning meaningfully.

aspects.title: Staff Well-being & Professional Climate
aspects.description: The school actively monitors and supports staff well-being. A professional climate characterised by recognition, manageable workload, low turnover, and a sense of shared purpose ensures that staff flourish alongside students.
expectations: Staff well-being is actively monitored through annual surveys and informal check-ins.
Workload management practices ensure staff sustainability — realistic meeting schedules, clear marking policies, and manageable expectations.
A culture of recognition exists — staff contributions are acknowledged formally and informally.
Staff turnover rate is monitored and is linked to satisfaction data and retention strategies.
Staff have access to professional support mechanisms — counselling, EAP, or peer support networks.
Staff are involved in key decisions affecting their professional lives and feel their voices are heard.
evidence_examples: Staff well-being survey results and follow-up action report
Staff/Employee Handbook (work conditions and support provisions)
Staff recognition records or awards programme
Staff turnover data and exit interview insights (anonymised)
Workload audit or staff feedback on workload sustainability
Staff meeting minutes showing staff involvement in school decisions

1.label: Beginning
1.description: Staff well-being is not monitored. Workload is unmanaged. No recognition programme exists. Staff turnover is high and causes are not investigated.

2.label: Developing
2.description: Staff well-being surveys are conducted but findings rarely lead to action. Some recognition exists but is informal. Workload concerns are acknowledged but not addressed systematically.

3.label: Established
3.description: Staff well-being is actively monitored and actions follow survey findings. A recognition culture is present. Workload is managed responsibly. Staff turnover is tracked and linked to retention strategies.

4.label: Exemplary
4.description: Staff well-being is a genuine school priority reflected in policy, culture, and practice. Staff satisfaction is high and turnover is low. The school is a place where professionals thrive. Staff voice is heard and drives meaningful change.
